data.title: SG Holdings: Still No Compelling Investment
data.description:
In October 2024, I initiated coverage for SG Holdings (OTCPK:SGHDY) (OTCPK:SGHHF) with a hold rating. The SGHDY ticker has remained flat, and that is due to the low volume. The listing in Japan (9143:JP) actually lost 6% of its value, which translated to US dollars would a 5.1% loss. The company recently announced its third quarter and nine months ended earnings for the year ending March 2025 and in this report I will be analyzing the earnings and updating my price target for the stock.

If you are not familiar with SG Holdings, you can read the description of the company that I provided in an earlier report.

SG Holdings: A Business Focused On Door-to-Door Delivery

SG Holdings is a Japanese company that operates through three segments, namely Delivery Business, Logistics Business, and Real Estate Business. During the year ending in March 2024, the company had net sales of ¥1,317 billion or $8.6 billion with net income of ¥58.3 billion or $380 million. It shows that while SG Holdings is likely not a familiar name to many, it is a company with billions of dollars in sales, sporting a market cap of over $6 billion.

The delivery business consists of traditional door-to-door deliveries but also increases transport services for corporate clients and vehicle management, freight loading and unloading, security, and cleaning services for large-scale facilities. The logistics business provides freight forwarding solutions and 3PL (third-party logistics) solutions. The real estate business includes the company's warehouses, which derive sales from leases.

Generally, driven by ecommerce, the trend for parcel volumes is directed upwards. However, at times there are also soft spots in the market and this year seems to be such a year with January parcel deliveries down 2.3% and year-to-date volumes are down almost 4%.

Expolanka Continues To Drive Sales Growth For SG Holdings

During the nine months ended, sales grew by 12.4% to ¥1.12 billion yen. The delivery business saw revenues decline by 0.6% to ¥779.7 million yen, driven by lower volumes partially offset by higher transportation management sales and higher unit prices per parcel. The logistics business saw a 73% increase in sales to ¥282.8 million due to higher cargo volumes and freight rates as Expolanka as well as the inclusion of Chilled & Frozen Logistics into the consolidated scope of the company. Excluding the addition of C&F, the sales would have been 54% higher. The real estate business saw sales growing 218.4% to ¥17.8 million, driven by the sale of real estate.

While revenues increased more than 12%, the operating income grew 7% this was driven by growth of the logistics business also increasing outsourcing expenses while depreciation and amortization, as well as other expenses, increased partially offset by lower personnel expenses. The consolidation of C&F also had a modest contribution to earnings after amortization of goodwill (purchasing price allocation).

SG Holdings Does Stock Is Still A Hold

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While SG Holdings has upside base on the company median EV/EBITDA and well as the peer group EV/EBITDA, to me, the investment case remains uncompelling. The reason is that the company's fundamental support for higher stock prices has declined since 2023 and while I do see 19% upside towards the price target for FY25, in the years after the upside is in the mid-single-digit range which is not enough to score a buy rating and I do believe that the company will largely depend on debt fueled M&A to grow the business at a higher rate.

Conclusion: SG Holdings Too Many Uncertainties

What holds for freight and logistics companies is that those companies had to rationalize after the golden times seen during the pandemic. With those times behind us and the global uncertainty on trade increasing on top of rising costs on labor and materials. I don't view the investment case for SG Holdings a compelling one. The stock is undervalued, but it seems to me that the expectations of debt fueled M&A is the primary reason for that, with little upside to the stock price beyond FY25. As a result, I am maintaining my hold rating.

data.title: Carvana Q4 Preview: Used Car Sales Are Booming
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) shares are up more than 50% since the last time I wrote on them in mid-January. A big driver behind this major increase has been the market shaking off the Hindenburg short report, which is something that, I thought, had little long run impact during my last research.

Now, just as I have voiced in each of my last two pieces of research, used car sales are turning up and to the right. This is obviously encouraging for Carvana, given that’s what the whole business is based on.

As more people return to office following the COVID-19 pandemic, it’s forcing people to finally upgrade their vehicles after years of making older cars last longer. With rising new car prices, more people are looking at used car sales. This is an incredible catalyst for Carvana.

Heading into earnings, I continue to believe shares are a strong buy.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

During my last piece of research, I explained how I thought Carvana had set a great foundation for 2025. Having renewed their key credit facility with Ally Bank, along with improving EPS numbers, have made me optimistic on the future of the company. I argued that they had more than 65% upside going forward.

Adding to this, the other key piece of the puzzle that’s been fairly solved for Carvana is the loan piece. Default rates have dropped. An auto loan default occurs if a customer’s payment is 30 or more days late. When this happens, customers may see a hit to their credit score or a repossession of the vehicle.

These default rates have plateaued, which is huge for underwriting auto loans (which is key to their business). According to Cox Auto, as of November, default rates were up 15.3% year-over-year as compared to November 2023, but the month-over-month rates were down 7.7% from October to November. Rates are plateauing, which is good for Carvana.

With this, the purpose of me doing follow-up coverage heading into earnings is to show the strong (and increasing) tail winds that Carvana has for 2025.

Why Used Car Sales Are Booming

Cox Auto reported on February 14th bullish used car sales which has me excited about the industry and Carvana in 2025. According to Cox Auto, there were 1.41 million used cars sold at retail during January, which was up 8% year-over-year.

The research also shows a forecast of 20.1 million retail used-vehicle sales in 2025, which, if met, would be a 1.2% increase as compared to 2024. To me, this 1.2% increase feels conservative given the strong start in January.

As Cox Auto and Auto Live Market view shows in the chart below, in the first full month of 2025, used-vehicle retail sales are already better than the first full month of each of the last three years. January 2025’s mark appears to be about 100,000 more than both 2024 and 2022.

However, it’s clear that the numbers fluctuate greatly. 2023 started off higher than both 2022 and 2024 but then finished below both years, I don’t think 2025 will follow the same path. It’s encouraging that 2025 has gotten off to a good start.

As I’ve mentioned already a few times in this article, I believe a big reason why sales are up is because of the fact that so many Americans are finally returning to in-person work following the COVID-19 pandemic.

There appears to be a cultural shift among employers to return to the office. President Trump signed an executive order entitled “return to in-person work,” for Federal employees. I think this trend is already in motion in the private sector (and now accelerating).

This is a huge tailwind Carvana. According to an article published by Forbes, full-time office workers spend an average of $1,020 per month on commuting expenses, which is more than double what hybrid workers pay. Carvana can grab a large chunk of this through used car sales.

Of course, a large majority of this monthly expense is things like gas and car repairs, but in order to maintain an in-office job, people need reliable transportation. Carvana should benefit.

As we see a steady uptick in people returning to the office, I think sales will continue to accelerate. People who have been working from home, perhaps with an unreliable (but tolerable) vehicle, will need to potentially buy a used car in order to get to work.

Q4 Preview

Carvana is set to report its Q4 EPS and revenue on Wednesday, February 19th after market close. On the earnings-per-share front, eight analysts project Carvana will post a total of $0.32. Seeking Alpha doesn't provide a year-over-year growth forecast for this projection because EPS was negative this time last year. So, I would say this would be pretty solid.

However, it’s interesting to note as we approach two days until earnings, that Carvana’s EPS projections wide apart. The same eight analysts estimate this EPS number to fall anywhere in between $0.07 and $0.74/share. I think this means there is room for shares to beat to the upside.

On the revenue front, Carvana should do well. According to 19 analysts, Carvana is estimated to post a Q4 revenue total of $3.34 billion. If this number is met, this would represent strong 37.94% year-over-year growth.

Carvana’s revisions over the past three months also are favorable. In the last three months, there have been three EPS revisions, with two of them being upward and one of them being downward. Similarly, out of 10 revenue revisions, seven have been upward, and only three have been downward. I think this shows most analysts are becoming increasingly optimistic about Carvana as we head toward earnings.

Valuation

In my last two pieces of research, I’ve really honed in on Carvana’s Non-GAAP P/E ratio. Each of the last two times I’ve written on Carvana, their Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio has been extremely higher than the sector median.

This research is no different. In fact, Carvana’s forward Non-GAAP P/E ratio is at an even higher premium to the sector median than it was in either of my two previous pieces of research. As of right now, Carvana has a forward Non-GAAP P/E of 192.63. This is a 1,024.99% premium to the sector median, which is just 17.12.

Seeking Alpha Quant grades Carvana as an F on this metric. Once again, this is not new information for investors who follow the used car giant.

But as I mentioned last time, I believe Carvana’s revenue growth more than makes up for this deficiency. Carvana has a forward revenue growth rate of 5.42%, which is 77.18% higher than the sector median of 3.06%. Seeking Alpha Quant gives Carvana a grade of a B on this metric.

As I argued last time, I still believe that Carvana should be trading at a 3x price-to-sales ratio. Their bottom line growth is still impressive (hence the expected 37.94% YoY expected bottom line growth in Q4), and I believe that overall growth will continue to excel as more people return to their office.

Right now, Carvana has a forward price-to-sales ratio of 2.72. If the company has a price-to-sales ratio that converges on the rate of 3x like I think they should, this would represent about 10.2% upside in shares.

Risks

I think the biggest risk for Carvana now is that used auto sales slowdown. As we saw from the chart earlier, we’ve seen a couple examples in recent years where growth starts strong but then fizzles out. In 2022 and 2023 especially, used car sales started out well but then tailed off near the end of the year.

I don’t think this will happen for Carvana. For me, a good majority of my belief hinges on return to office mandates like Trump’s executive order, which I believe will cause used car sales to rise. I think this will be enough to break the trend.

Helping to hedge this, Carvana has a very strong inventory turnover ratio, which means the used auto retailer can adjust their inventory needs to customer expectations if the return to office trend changes. According to Seeking Alpha, Carvana’s asset turnover ratio is currently at 1.74, which is significantly higher than the sector median.

I think these two things will continue to be tailwinds for the company.

Bottom Line

I think Carvana is set up for a strong 2025 and a strong Q4 report later this week. With Trump’s return to office executive order, I think used car dealers (like Carvana) have undeniable tailwinds. Used car sales are already off to a good start in 2025. In fact, other used car dealers like Penske Automotive have reported strong Q4’s (their revenue hit a record in 2024). I think the writing is on the wall for a solid quarter for Carvana.

With this, shares have more room to run. That’s why I continue to believe shares are a strong buy going into Wednesday’s earnings print.

data.title: Weitz Core Plus Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
The Core Plus Income Fund’s Institutional Class returned -2.30% for the fourth quarter compared to a -3.06% return for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (Agg), bringing calendar year returns to +3.37% and +1.25% respectively. Longer-term results (3-, 5-, and 10-year) continue to outpace the index (see performance table on page 4). The Fund’s outperformance for the year was driven primarily by sector and security selection, led by collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), asset-backed securities (ABS) and corporate bonds.

Overview

While there were many surprises in 2024, one of the biggest could be what occurred in the U.S. Treasury bond market. It was the tale of two time periods: the time before the Federal Reserve (Fed) decisively cut short-term interest rates on September 18—and the time after. Leading up to September 18, Treasury interest rates declined across the yield curve by more than one-half of a percent in 2- and 3-year Treasuries. But after September 18, and despite two additional short-term interest rate cuts by the Fed, interest rates increased meaningfully across the yield curve. For example, 10-year Treasuries increased nearly a full percent (87 basis points) from the Fed’s first cut on September 18 through year-end.

Why is this a surprise? As the graph below illustrates (using the 10-year Treasury), it is not common for bond yields to increase—particularly so markedly—following the start of a short-term interest-rate easing (lowering) cycle.

According to Jim Bianco of Bianco Research, the current easing cycle, as measured by the market reaction in 10-year bond yields 100 days after the first cut, has recorded the second-largest increase among the 12 Fed rate-cutting campaigns since 1963, with only the 1981 cycle experiencing a greater surge. Threats of tariffs and their potential inflationary effects from the incoming administration, economic resilience, and uncertainty around fiscal policy are often cited as reasons for the atypical move in interest rates amid a rate cutting cycle. It could also reflect the bond market reacting to a Fed that, after infamously deeming inflation “transitory” a few years ago, may now be prematurely claiming victory over inflation.

Portfolio Positioning

The table on the next page shows the change in allocation to various sectors, from the prior quarter and from the prior year. This summary provides a view over time of how we have allocated capital. Since our goal is to invest in sectors that we believe offer the best risk-adjusted returns, our allocations may change significantly over time.

1 Corporate CLOs are included in the ABS segment in the Fund's schedule of investments but are additionally called out separately for the purposes of the discussion.

2 High-Yield exposure (as of 12/31/2024) consists of investments in the Corporate, Corporate Convertible, ABS and CMBS sectors. 3 Corporate CLOs and CMBS may include private credit investments.

The most notable increase in sector allocation during the fourth quarter (and for the calendar year) was an increase in mortgage-backed securities (MBS). Prior to 2023, the Fund had not had a meaningful investment in MBS. Since that time, we have steadily increased our allocation to over 30%. Our focus has been and remains higher coupon agency MBS, with coupons of 4.50%-6.50%. Agency MBS is an area within benchmark categories that has rarely looked more attractive relative to investment grade corporate bonds. Low investment grade spreads can make a lot of alternatives look good but the high-quality nature of agency MBS and what we believe is a better return profile relative to investment grade corporate bonds remain a positive investment theme for our investment portfolios.

In addition to MBS, we added Treasury securities, principally in 10–20-year maturities. We also added a wide variety of securitized investments, including investments within Auto ABS, CLOs, CMBS, and whole business.

In terms of overall portfolio metrics, the Fund’s average effective maturity increased to 9.5 years as of December 31, 2024, from 9.0 years as of September 30, 2024, while our average effective duration increased to 5.8 years from 5.5 years over the same period, compared to the Agg’s average effective duration of 6.0 years on December 31. These measures provide a guide to the Fund’s interest rate sensitivity. A higher average effective duration increases the Fund’s price sensitivity to changes in interest rates (either up or down).

As of December 31, our high-yield exposure as a percentage of net assets stayed at 3.9%, similarly to September 30, 2024. The Fund can invest up to 25% of net assets in high yield, therefore we have ample capacity to take advantage of valuation discrepancies/ opportunities in the high yield area.

The Fund’s yield to worst (YTW) remains higher than the Bloomberg Barclays U.S. Aggregate index, while the Fund’s interest rate risk (duration) continues to be lower. Constructing a portfolio that produces “carry” – income achieved from regular coupon payments – (via the YTW measurement) and lower interest rate sensitivity (via lower duration) is a feature of our flexible mandate and ability to invest in other higher quality securities that reside outside of the benchmark. The Fund’s 30-day SEC yield was 5.18% (subsidized) and 5.14% (unsubsidized) as of December 31, 2024 (subsidized yield reflects fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements, while unsubsidized yield does not).

Top Quarterly Contributors

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): CLO were the top contributor to performance due to strong coupon income and modest price appreciation due to tighter spreads

Asset Backed Securities: Our ABS portfolio was a positive contributor to performance due to high coupon income and price appreciation, as ABS spreads tightened throughout the year.

Top Quarterly Detractors

U.S. Treasury Bonds: U.S. Treasuries were the largest detractor to performance. With a duration of over 10 years, our Treasury portfolio experienced unrealized mark-to-market losses as interest rates rose significantly during the quarter.

Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS): Agency MBS was the second-largest detractor to performance as interest rates rose and mortgage-backed spreads widened during the quarter.

Corporate Bonds: Corporate bonds were a slight detractor to performance as higher interest rates more than offset coupon income and modest spread tightening during the quarter.

Fund Strategy Revisited

Our approach consists primarily of investing in a diversified portfolio of high-quality bonds while maintaining an overall portfolio average duration of 3.5 to 7 years. We may seek to capture attractive coupon income and potential price appreciation by investing in longer-duration and lower-quality bonds when attractively priced. We may also invest up to 25% in fixed income securities that are not considered investment grade (such as high yield and convertible bonds as well as preferred and convertible preferred stock), and we do so when we perceive the risk/reward characteristics to be favorable.

We do not, and will not, try to mimic any particular index as we construct our portfolio. We believe our flexible mandate and high-conviction portfolio will benefit investors over the long term. We utilize a bottom-up, research-driven approach and select portfolio assets one security at a time based on our view of opportunities in the marketplace. Our fixed income research is not dependent on, but often benefits from, the due diligence work our equity teammates conduct on companies and industries.

Overall, we strive to be adequately compensated for the risks assumed to maximize investment (or reinvestment) yield and to avoid making interest rate bets, particularly those that depend on interest rates going down. We have often maintained a lower duration profile than the index, particularly in very low-yield environments. Our shorter duration profile has benefited shareholders in periods of rising interest rates.

Maintaining a liquid (e.g. Treasuries and agency MBS) and overall, up-in-quality portfolio positioning that we believe can generate higher income returns than respective indexes position us well as we begin a new year a year that could get messy in so many unpredictable ways. But also, it could be one that will particularly allow us to lean into and be liquidity providers if/when more favorable risk/reward opportunities present themselves.

We are thankful for the trust and partnership many have in our firm and its investment process. 2025: a New Year…a new blank page…. and renewed commitment to keep earning our investment partner’s trust.

data.title: Richtech Robotics: Sell On Poor Results And Elevated Valuation
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Richtech Robotics Inc. or “Richtech” (NASDAQ:RR) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier article on the company.

At the beginning of the year, I initiated coverage of robotic solutions provider Richtech Robotics with a "Sell" rating due to an ugly combination of elevated business risks, persistent dilution and poor shareholder communication.

Since that time, the company has filed its annual report on form 10-K for FY2024 and its quarterly report on form 10-Q for Q1/FY2025.

As usual, Richtech did not issue an earnings press release and abstained from holding a conference call, thus leaving investors largely in the dark with regard to its financial performance.

However, taking a closer look at the company's financial results explains management's reluctance to communicate with investors on this topic:

While sales were up slightly on a year-over-year basis, revenues of just $1.3 million aren't exactly a reason to celebrate. However, the company's gross margin advanced to a new all-time high of 90.2% as the recent shift to a leasing model (Robotics-as-a-Service or "RaaS") has resulted in both revenues and related costs being recognized over the lease term.

While management has stated a number of reasons for the business model change, like creating recurring revenue streams, stronger customer relationships and reduced sales cycles, it is important to note that the Richtech is not a software company.

In fact, Richtech is trying to sell expensive robotic solutions with a focus on the hospitality and service industry.

Given the requirement for substantial upfront investments with unclear return profiles, customers have been hesitant to adopt the company's robotic solutions in recent years.

To address the issue, Richtech has not only decided to offer lease financing to potential customers, the company has also committed to operate 20 Walmart-located restaurants in Arizona, Colorado, and Texas under a franchise agreement with Ghost Kitchens America's "One Kitchen" brand.

According to the disclosures made in the 10-Q, the company has opened its first One Kitchen store in a Walmart location near Chicago last month.

One Kitchen offers a unique and interactive dining experience, showcasing the potential of our robotic technology to transform the food service industry. We anticipate opening additional One Kitchen stores near Atlanta and San Diego in late spring 2025.

Two weeks ago, Richtech also opened its first "Clouffee & Tea" store in Las Vegas:

Our first Clouffee & Tea store opened its doors in February 2025, marking our entry into the rapidly growing market for automated coffee and tea service. Clouffee & Tea combines our advanced robotics technology with a curated selection of premium beverages, creating a unique and engaging customer experience. We are confident in the potential of this brand and plan to open additional Clouffee & Tea locations in the coming months.

Last year, Richtech raised $19.4 million in net proceeds from a combined stock and warrant offering priced at $1.35 per share.

Following the meteoric rise of the company's shares over the past two months, Richtech has raised substantial, additional proceeds from warrant exercises.

However, common shareholder dilution wasn't limited to warrant exercises. During the quarter, the company issued an additional 4.1 million shares for unspecified "technology development services", 3.4 million shares to the "employee and director incentive pool" and 0.4 million shares to "advisors and consultants as compensation for services rendered".

As a result, outstanding shares have increased to 114.5 million.

Last week, Richtech agreed to issue 2.7 million new warrants with an exercise price of $4.00 to a fund managed by Armistice Capital in exchange for exercising 2.7 million existing warrants at a price of $1.35 for gross proceeds of $3.6 million.

The company also entered into a settlement agreement with AC Sunshine Securities LLC, pursuant to which Richtech agreed to pay $430,000.

Even when considering the settlement payment and assuming additional cash usage, I would estimate Richtech's current cash balance to be well above $40 million.

That said, the company's cash usage is likely to increase further due to a number of issues:

  • significantly increased investments in sales and marketing
  • requirement to provide lease financing to customers
  • material expansion of the company's One Kitchen and Clouffee & Tea store fleet

An assumed cash burn rate of $5 million per quarter would translate into approximately two years of runway for Richtech. Given this issue, I wouldn't be surprised to see the company returning to the capital markets in the not-too-distant-future.

For my part, I continue to dislike the elevated risks and substantial cash drain resulting from the recent change in the company's business model and decision to operate restaurants and coffee stores at scale.

Depending on the pace of additional store openings, Richtech's hospitality revenue will eclipse the company's core robotics business sooner rather than later, which is likely to negatively impact gross margins, particularly given the fact that Richtech has no experience in operating restaurants.

Even when generously assuming FY2025 sales to reach $10 million, the company is currently trading at a revenue multiple of more than 30x, which apparently doesn't make much sense.

In addition, with NVIDIA (NVDA) having recently disposed of its stake in Serve Robotics (SERV), the recent robotics hype might come to an end soon as momentum traders leave for assumed greener pastures:

Considering the ugly combination of elevated valuation, persistent dilution, poor investor communications and very risky business model, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on Richtech Robotics' common shares.

Risks

A continuation of the recent robotics hype could result in momentum traders chasing the shares aggressively again.

Bottom Line

Richtech Robotics' investor communications continue to leave much to be desired, as evidenced by the fact that the company does neither issue quarterly earnings press releases nor hold conference calls.

However, a closer look at Richtech's recent SEC filings reveals a story of persistent dilution and material cash burn resulting from recent business model changes.

At this point, it is difficult to envision the company growing into its elevated valuation anytime soon if ever.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on Richtech Robotics' common shares.

data.title: February's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 10.49%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 433 stocks at this time from the 423 listed last month. I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by forward dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions and I try to note those.

Below, we go over a quick initial look at the top five highest rankings according to dividend yield. However, as is usually the case, we have some duplicates every month, and those that we have touched on recently that we'll be skipping over include NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP), NETSTREIT Corp. (NTST) and United Parcel Service (UPS).

We also have names that come up on the list that doesn't really necessarily meet the spirit of the screening piece in terms of consistent growth over time. This month, that means we'll be skipping over The Western Union (WU)—a company that has continued to struggle with newer competition and has now gone four full years without a dividend bump.

With that said, here are the names that we will be touching on today: Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), Enbridge (ENB), Edison International (EIX), NNN REIT (NNN) and VICI Properties (VICI). Three of these names are REITs, and the other two are utility/infrastructure names. These are naturally names that come with relatively higher yields, and are certainly interesting being an income-focused investor.

Innovative Industrial Properties - 10.49% Yield

IIPR is a popular REIT in the industrial cannabis space, of which there aren't too many publicly traded options for investors. This is the only one that trades on the NYSE as well. It offers a tempting yield, which is similar to its significantly smaller peer, NLCP—which we touched on just last month. However, these companies come with higher relative risks in terms of regulations.

More specifically, IIPR has found itself in a bit of trouble with another tenant having issues and defaulting on its rent. Not just any tenant, either; it's the largest tenant in terms of the annual base rent. So that comes with a huge overhanging risk in terms of what they may have to do with the dividend going forward.

Though they also recently announced a resolution with PharmaCann, but have reduced cumulative total base rent and are working with PharmaCann to find new tenants for two of the leased properties. If no new tenant is found by August 1, 2025, then IIPR "will regain full control." The "monthly base rent of $1.3 million will be abated in full effective February 1, 2025."

The REIT had already seen its growth slow significantly, and that meant its distribution growth had slowed to a crawl. Of course, given the above, AFFO is expected to take a hit here. Analysts are estimating that 2024 AFFO will come to $8.04 and 2025 will decline to $7.72.

Based on the latest $1.90 quarterly, we would be looking at a payout ratio of almost 98.5% for 2025 if they deliver analysts' estimates. That's an incredibly tight coverage, and that could see them reduce the payout in order to keep some cushion for cash flow. If any further issues crop up in the near term, that almost nearly guarantees a dividend reduction.

I currently hold a position, but it certainly is a highly speculative one at this time if they can maintain the current dividend to investors. At the same time, some of the best performances that investors can achieve are in those names that present the highest risks. There has since been some recovery in the share price, so there appears to be some optimism here. Earnings are coming up on February 19, which should provide us with some further updates.

Enbridge - 6.17% Yield

ENB is an income-investor favorite, which needs little introduction, as this name pops up regularly on this monthly publication. This would be thanks to its long history of paying a consistently growing dividend. The company is located in Canada and is primarily known for its pipeline business due to the size of that business. However, it wouldn't be incorrect to label this company as more of an infrastructure company, as it works through several lines of business.

That includes a utility business that it has been working on growing through acquisitions, which have closed as of the latest quarterly results.

Our leverage is within our target range of 4.5 to 5x debt-to-EBITDA after closing PSNC, and we expect to trend down over the next few quarters. With the acquisitions closed and the funding completed, we have successfully concluded the acquisition of 3 U.S. gas utilities, which perfectly fit Enbridge's low-risk business model. And so we have returned to an equity self-funded model. This has been a well-executed major transaction that investors will benefit from for years to come.

For U.S. investors, it may not seem that dividend growth is consistent due to the CAD to USD conversion and the U.S. dollar strengthening.

However, they have been continuing to deliver dividend growth. In fact, the latest announcement was in December, where they provided a 3% bump; that took the dividend from $0.915 per quarter to $0.9425. They can also boast that they've paid dividends to their shareholders for going on 70 years now.

Edison International - 5.87% Yield

I had considered not including EIX because it is another highly speculative name currently in the utility space due to the Los Angeles Eaton fire disaster, where the company is being investigated. If it is found that they were responsible, we know how bad it can get for these utility companies. They then later admitted that their equipment may have sparked the fire.

More specifically, we have experience with California when the utility company PG&E (PCG) declared bankruptcy and reorganized due to liability claims for their role in a previous fire tragedy.

Although the equity here wasn't wiped out, which is unusual for a bankruptcy, shareholders certainly were hurting, nonetheless. The dividend was suspended and only brought back to a token penny in late 2023, and it was increased recently, but still remains a token 0.64% yield. That is even after PCG shares have been tanking recently, along with EIX's, due to the latest fires.

So, to be clear, this is a highly speculative position at this point, but they had otherwise been a steady dividend raiser, and the latest increase came with the last announcement.

They've set their next earnings announcement to take place on February 27, and investors might want to wait for more details at that time.

NNN REIT - 5.85% Yield

Speaking of defaults and bankruptcy, we arrive at NNN, which finds some of its tenants also in trouble. However, it isn't as serious of an issue since this REIT is overall more diversified, with these tenants being a smaller portion of the annual base rent. When talking about Frisch's and Conn's, here are some details they included on their previous earnings call.

Both of these tenants are on cash basis accounting, so we are only recording what we actually collect. Over the years, our guidance has generally assumed 100 basis points of rent loss in any given period but these 2 tenant issues could push us over that level, obviously, in the fourth quarter. We should get some incremental clarity on these 2 situations in the fourth quarter, which we think will allow us to better estimate the outcomes when we provide our 2025 guidance in February.

That was in the previous earnings call, but their latest earnings report showed that these issues were being resolved, which helped the shares rally higher post-earnings.

NNN has initiated eviction proceedings for 64 properties leased to a mid-western restaurant operator. As of December 31, 2024, NNN had taken back possession of 33 of those properties of which 28 properties have been re-leased to another restaurant operator with rent commencing May 1, 2025. NNN is working to take possession of the remainder of the properties in the first quarter of 2025. Additionally, during the fourth quarter of 2024, NNN took possession of 32 properties previously leased to a southeast U.S. furniture retailer that had filed for bankruptcy. As of December 31, 2024, NNN had sold six of these properties generating net proceeds of $21.8 million and re-leased five of these properties.

This is another name that I currently hold, and I consider it one of my core portfolio positions. I wrote some puts into this weakness to potentially capitalize on building out my position further, or generating some cash flow. These puts expire on March 21, 2025, and we selected the $40 strike price, collecting $1.39 in option premium (we originally sold these puts on January 3rd.)

NNN had already been trading well below its historical average range. That isn't that out of the ordinary for most REITs as we've shifted to a higher-rate environment. Due to persistently sticky inflation and tariffs, it looks like it'll be set to continue with rate cuts being minimal in 2025 after the Fed began cutting rates in late 2024. Still, the latest sell-off has pushed it further into a territory where I believe it is attractively valued.

VICI Properties - 5.69% Yield

Another name I currently own, VICI, is REIT, which owns experiential assets. They are fairly concentrated in terms of their largest contributors to the annual base rent. They primarily own casino properties, but they have been looking to branch out a bit more.

Fortunately, there don't seem to be any red flags going up on this one. At least, no specific tenant issues or investigations are taking place, as we noted with some others here. Though some investors may be disappointed in the lack of appreciation in the share price over the years, it continues to deliver a solid yield. Even further, during a recessionary period, most REITs are going to be susceptible, but the underlying businesses here aren't normally as impacted. During the Covid crash, AFFO here continued to grow at a steady clip.

This is a name we regularly utilize this name for the options wheel strategy to squeeze further cash flow from this name aside from collecting some dividends occasionally. That is, writing puts until potential assignment and then turning around and writing covered calls. We are currently in the covered calls stage of the process with the March 21, 2025 expiration, where we nabbed $0.85 in premium. Counting this latest premium collected, we've brought in $2.72 from this latest batch of assigned shares, where we took assignment on June 21, 2024.

Given this, VICI being relatively stable in terms of its share price floating around $30 to $35 works fairly well for us employing this strategy.

data.title: Orsted Grows Into A Wind Giant
data.description:
Ørsted (OTCPK:DNNGY) (OTCPK:DOGEF) is one of the largest international energy companies, and the largest one in Denmark, worth $16 billion. It also recently got a new shareholder, Equinor (EQNR), one of the largest energy companies globally, which spent $2.3 billion for a 10% stake at higher prices. With recent weakness, as we'll see throughout this article, the company is a unique investment opportunity.

Ørsted 2024 Results

Ørsted had a strong 2024 with more than $3 billion in EBITDA along with cash flow from net reversal of cancellation fees.

Still, while the company managed to achieve a double-digit ROCE, it faced substantial impairment losses. This is especially true in relation to its US offshore wind portfolio. With President Trump launching an executive order to freeze offshore wind, the company could see continued struggles with its U.S. offshore wind portfolio.

Ørsted 2030 Vision

The company has walked back its 2030 vision and investment by ~25%. That means it'll invest $10 billion USD less by 2030 than it initially planned too ($30 billion investment plan).

The company now expects to invest roughly 220 billion DKK ($30 billion USD) in the 7-year period, or roughly $4.15 billion USD per year. Out of that number, the company already spent $6 billion USD in 2024 and the company's share for projects already under construction is $18 billion. So the company is dramatically cutting capital expenditures towards the end of the decade.

Ørsted Short-Term Buildout

A result of this aggressive investment is the company building an incredibly strong portfolio in the short term.

The company has 18.2 gigawatts of production capacity, and it has more than 9 gigawatts under construction. The company will have more than 27 gigawatts in 2027, which at $0.1 / KWh is $2.7 million / operational hour. It's 50% expansion in the company's capacity, and it doesn't count a number of other near-term opportunities.

The scale of the company's operations is visible above. It's worth noting that President Trump has instituted a complete ban on U.S. offshore wind, which could threaten some of the projects and planned projects above that are planned such as Revolution. However, these projects (Revolution and Sunrise) already have all federal permitting in place.

The company's massive portfolio of offshore wind here, most of which is under construction, shows the true scale of its portfolio and the opportunities.

Ørsted Financial Targets

The net result here is strong financial growth for the company, with 12% EBITDA growth expected annualized from 2024-2026.

The net result here is 31 DKK bn in 2026 EBITDA or $4.3 billion USD. That's quite strong for a $16 billion market cap company (USD). The company has continued to earn a double-digit return on capital employed and plans to continue those growth targets. The company's EBITDA as it ramps down spending will enable a shift to shareholder returns.

The company's growth will mainly come from sites and existing partnership. The company will also see onshore and bioenergy support. As the company ramps up a number of impressive sites here, the company should be able to increase its earnings.

Our View

Ørsted is continuing to invest in its portfolio and long-term shareholder returns.

The company plans to use partnerships, CFFO, and debt, etc. to earn this capital spending. This is money that the company can comfortably afford without too much debt or anything similar. However, there is an important takeaway here, which is that the company will produce no direct shareholder returns until the 2030s. It's investing in its business.

The company has ~$8 billion USD in net interest-bearing debt; however, it's something that the company can comfortably afford with its $16 billion market cap and continued cash flow. The company's climate focused portfolio has enabled it to issue primarily green and blue bonds, as visible here, which means its weighted interest rates are just a few %.

That means the company's interest rate costs are just a few hundred $ million. The company can comfortably afford that, while continuing its growth, towards adjusted EBITDA at more than $4 billion annualized. Versus the company's market cap, it's enough to enable double-digit shareholder returns.

The company had -$1.3 billion USD FY 2024 FCF, but that was after with almost $6 billion USD in net investment, or more than 30% of its market cap. Once those massive investments are removed, the company can drive strong shareholder returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is valuation. The company is worth $16 billion and while EBITDA is impressive, it's all being reinvested back into the company's business. That means it could be a number of years, into the 2030s, before shareholders see any noteworthy returns, which means patience is essential.

Conclusion

Ørsted is a clean energy company with an exciting future. The company is well positioned for the rapid growth of low-cost wind energy, and these are assets with a multi-decade timeframe. At the same time, the company is able to finance this growth with incredibly low-cost green bonds. That combination is quite valuable.

The company is redirecting all cash to growth. That's in the immediate term. However, after that, the company will be able to use its FCF to generate strong shareholder returns. That makes the company a valuable long-term investment for patient investors. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Microsoft: Still An Attractive Growth Juggernaut
data.description:
With a market capitalization as of this writing of $3.05 trillion, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) is a true behemoth in the stock market. It's one of the largest publicly traded companies the world has ever known and even most people who don't invest in the market are certainly aware of its existence. In fact, odds are that if you have a computer, you use at least one of its services. Globally, for instance, an estimated 72% of all desktop computers have Microsoft Windows. And this doesn't even factor in the different programs or apps that you use that are probably hosted on its cloud network. I guess you could say that Microsoft is omnipresent.

Back in early November of last year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. For those who follow me closely, this might seem like a peculiar decision. After all, I am a value investor at heart. And there is no denying that shares of Microsoft are anything but cheap. However, despite the firm's large size, it continues growing rapidly. Add on top of this its market leading position in the cloud computing industry, a space that is forecasted to grow from $496 billion in 2023 to $2 trillion by 2030, which translates to an annual compound rate of 22%, and the upside for the company moving forward is massive.

Unfortunately, since the time that article was published, shares are down 1.7% at a time in the S&P 500 is up 0.9%. But underperformance is something that has become rather normal for the enterprise. Over the last year, shares are virtually flat. However, I believe that the rapid growth of its cloud operations, combined with plans to further grow this part of the pie, will ultimately result in additional upside as time goes on. Because of this, I believe that keeping the company rated a soft ‘buy’ makes sense right now.

Check out that growth

When I last wrote about Microsoft last year, investors had access to data covering through the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Results now extend through the second quarter. During that time, revenue for the company totaled a whopping $69.63 billion. This represents an increase of 8.8% compared to the $64.02 billion the business generated one year earlier. To best understand the firm, however, it is best to dig into its individual operations. From a purely operational standpoint, the company really does have only three business segments.

The first of these is its largest, which is its Productivity and Business Processes segment. This is the part of the company that includes things such as Office 365, Microsoft Teams, Copilot Pro, LinkedIn, and more. During the second quarter of 2025, revenue for this unit came in at $29.44 billion. This happens to be 13.9% above the $25.85 billion generated the previous year. In the chart above, you can also see results for this segment for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. During this time, revenue expanded by 13.1% from $51.08 billion to $57.75 billion.

The growth for this unit was driven by multiple factors. For instance, LinkedIn revenue grew by 9% year over year for the most recent quarter. Microsoft 365 Commercial products and related cloud services saw growth of 15%. But the story here is not just about the top line. It's also about the bottom line. While revenue grew nicely, segment profits popped up 16.3%. That took the metric from $14.52 billion to $16.89 billion. And for the first half of 2025, profits for this unit grew by 15.9% from $28.81 billion to $33.40 billion.

It's honestly not shocking to me to see profitability grow faster than revenue. This is a service-oriented business that is achieving greater economies of scale as it expands. The economic moat that its products are building make it almost a guarantee that margins will improve. For the most recent quarter, the profit margin associated with this segment was 57.4%. That compares to the 56.1% experienced one year earlier. This might not sound like much. But when applied to just the annualized revenue achieved during the most recent quarter, this difference would work out to an extra $3.62 billion in pre-tax profits annually.

Before I get to what I believe is the most exciting part of the company, I should touch on what I consider to be the least exciting. This would be its More Personal Computing segment. In essence, this is a collection of all the miscellaneous operations that don't fit well under its other two operating segments. Examples here include Windows, sales of different devices such as the Surface, all of its gaming operations such as Xbox and Activision Blizzard, and various search and news advertising services such as Bing and Microsoft News.

During the most recent quarter, revenue for this segment was $14.65 billion. That was almost flat compared to the $14.64 billion generated the previous year. This is actually surprising when you consider that for the first half of 2025, the $27.83 billion generated by the segment ended up being 7.4% above the $25.92 billion reported the previous year. For the entire fiscal year so far, this segment has benefited from a growth in certain areas, such as a $149 million increase in Windows and devices revenue and a 2% rise in Xbox content and services revenue that management attributed to growth involving the firm’s Xbox Game Pass. Search and news advertising revenue jumped an impressive $390 million, which amounted to roughly 12% year over year, as the company benefited from higher search volume and higher revenue per search.

The problem for this segment seems to have been related to video game consoles. Year over year in the second quarter, which would correspond with the quarter ending at the end of December, management said that overall console revenue dropped 29% because of lower volumes. This is not terribly surprising. Last year, I wrote an article about GameStop (GME) and detailed in it how, for the first 10 months of that year, US console sales were down 28.7%. There are multiple reasons for this. For instance, both the Xbox Series and PlayStation 5 consoles are actually now quite dated, having been released back in 2020. So many of the people who want a console already have one. You also have the rise of subscription services, such as Microsoft’s Game Pass, that are reducing the need to buy consoles. Furthermore, you have a rapidly growing mobile game market.

Obviously, Microsoft will benefit in other ways such as through its own video game titles and the aforementioned Game Pass. But that doesn't make the $530 million decline in Gaming revenue, which was caused by the 29% drop in Xbox hardware revenue that was seen just in a single quarter on a year over year basis any less bad. For context, I did end up going back and looking at overall sales associated with Xbox devices for last year compared to the previous year. As you can see in the table above, overall volumes were down 33.7%, with much of that heavily weighted near the very end of the year.

One thing I am happy to see is that, even though revenue remained virtually flat in the most recent quarter, segment profits actually managed to grow 32.2% from $2.96 billion to $3.92 billion. Management said that this was driven by lower operating expenses involving its gaming operations, and by a surge in gross margin associated with gaming, search, news advertising, Windows, and devices sales that management attributed to a change in product mix in favor of higher margin businesses and operational improvements elsewhere such as under the search and news advertising part of the pie.

Finally, we get to the most exciting part of the company. And this would be its Intelligent Cloud business, which involves its public, private, and hybrid server products and related cloud services. During the most recent quarter, revenue for this unit came in at $25.54 billion. That happens to be 18.9% above the $21.53 billion generated the previous year. And for the first nine months of last year, the $49.64 billion generated by the segment ended up being 19.5% greater than the $41.54 billion generated one year earlier. Management claimed that Azure and other cloud services revenue jumped a whopping 31% year over year. AI services revenue specifically skyrocketed 157%. For the first half of 2025, Azure growth was even greater at 32%, with AI services revenue climbing 178%.

But again, this is not all about the revenue side of the equation. Operating profits for this unit came in at $10.85 billion. This happened to be 13.6% above what it was the previous year. And for the first half of 2025, profits grew by 15.7% from $18.46 billion to $21.35 billion. To be honest with you, this year over year improvement in profits, which resulted in a decline in profit margin, was a little disappointing to see. However, management attributed this because of major investments being made in the company's AI infrastructure.

This is the kind of margin contraction that you want to see if you have to see it. It is essentially accepting short term pain for long term gain. It's also important to note that this is not uncharacteristic for Microsoft. As the chart above illustrates, profit margins actually contracted between 2021 and 2023 for this unit. They did surge in 2024. But so far for 2025, they are down compared to the previous year. In all likelihood, this weakness will continue in the near term. After all, during its second quarter earnings release, the company revealed that it is planning to invest $80 billion in AI enabled data centers this year alone. Over half of that spending is expected to be in the US. At the start of this article, I made clear just how big the opportunity in this space is. And as one of the only two clear market leaders in the space, the other being Amazon (AMZN) with its AWS platform, the future for the company appears bright.

On the bottom line, 2025 is looking up as well. As the very first chart in this article illustrates, net profits in the most recent quarter came in at $24.11 billion. That's 10.2% above the $21.87 billion generated the previous year. Operating cash flow grew from $18.85 billion to $22.29 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get an increase from $29.15 billion to $33.84 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company grew from $32.99 billion to $38.48 billion. In the chart above, you can also see results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, all grew nicely year over year.

Before I get into valuing the company, one other thing I would like to touch on briefly would be what management is doing for shareholders besides just focusing on growth. During the most recent quarter, the company repurchased $4.73 billion worth of shares. This was net of share issuances that seemed to have been used for rewarding insiders. This was up from the $3.74 billion, on a net basis, reported the previous year. And for the first half of 2025, the $8.13 billion spending beat out the $7.89 billion spent for the first half of 2024. Investors should fully expect this kind of growth to continue. Under its $60 billion share buyback program launched in November of 2021, the company still has $4 billion remaining. However, in September of last year, the firm also announced a new $60 billion share buyback program.

This wasn't all. Management has also been paying out dividends. The $6.17 billion paid out in the second quarter of 2025 was higher than the $5.57 billion paid out the previous year. And year over year for the first half of the year, we saw an increase from $10.63 billion to $11.74 billion. It is worth mentioning that management has continued to make major investments in growth while also returning such tremendous amounts of capital to shareholders without sacrificing the firm's balance sheet. Excluding long term investments that are on its books the amount of $15.58 billion, the company also has a net cash position of $26.59 billion.

Management has not really been all that detailed when it comes to what investors should expect for 2025 as a whole. They have said that revenue should grow at a double digit rate. But in the absence of more detailed estimates, the best thing that we can do is really to annualize the results we have seen so far. Doing this, we would get net profits of $97.35 billion, adjusted operating cash flow of $136.78 billion, and EBITDA of $157.75 billion. Using these estimates, as well as historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. On an absolute basis, this is certainly not a value play. But when you consider that this is an industry-leading cash cow with tremendous growth potential, even if we ignore growth outside of the cloud computing market, it's difficult not to be at least slightly bullish. In the table below, I also compared the company to five similar firms. And using each of the three valuation scenarios, it ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Microsoft (MSFT) 31.3 22.3 19.2
Oracle Corporation (ORCL) 43.4 24.9 24.9
ServiceNow (NOW) 150.0 50.1 87.7
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) 51.0 42.9 90.3
CrowdStrike Holdings (CRWD) 836.8 76.8 254.4
Fortinet (FTNT) 48.9 37.8 36.1

Risks to be mindful of

Naturally, there are some risks here that investors should be aware of. The first of these is that, even though Microsoft is a growth juggernaut, it's not the cheapest company as I highlighted already. As a value investor, I tend to prefer businesses that are trading at low multiples, usually under 10 times cash flow. But here we are with a company that, on a forward basis for 2025 even, is trading at a price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple of 22.3. In the event that growth falls short of analysts’ expectations, downside could be in the cards.

There is also industry-specific and economic-specific risk. On the industry side of things, Total investments in cloud computing this year are forecasted to be $353 billion. That's 32% above what it was the previous year. If overinvestment does occur, it could ultimately lead to weakness in this space that would harm the firms that benefit from the rapid growth and significant margins associated with the industry. On the economic side of things, it is also worth pointing out that the IMF (International Monetary Fund) is forecasting global growth of only 3.6% both this year and next year.

That's below the 3.7% annualized growth experienced in the 10 years prior to the COVID-19 pandemic. China, in particular, is slowing down. And if we add on top of this the prospect of tariffs that could be implemented, further growth revisions downward could be in store. Slower growth means that investments in this space might not be as rewarding. And when you add on top of this sticky inflation in some parts of the world such as the US, then the end result of higher interest rates aimed at bringing inflation down could make expansion more costly. That would naturally weigh on growth as well.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I remain bullish about Microsoft. From a shareholder return perspective, the last year has not been exactly great. But this is a tremendous company with a great track record. Growth has been strong and future prospects look positive. Add on top of this a solid balance sheet and how the company is rewarding shareholders while also focusing on growth, and I do believe that it warrants a soft ‘buy’ even in spite of how pricey the stock might look on an absolute basis.

data.title: No Pricing Power, No Profitability: CSP Is A Struggling Niche Player In Need Of Reinvention
data.description:
Technology solutions provider, CSP Inc. (NASDAQ:CSPI) is at a strategic crossroads, with its revenue in decline and sustainable profitability hard to attain. The firm faces enormous headwinds, and, given its size, innovation constraints, and commitment to a low-margin business, the current round of losses will prove sticky. The business is in urgent need of a strategic shift. For the risk-averse investors, CSP is not for them. CSP will likely be a loss maker for a long time.

The Business Model

The Lowell, Massachusetts-based technology solutions provider has, since its founding in 1968, positioned itself as a niche player in IT infrastructure, cybersecurity, and managed IT services (MSP). The firm operates through two segments: Technology Solutions (TS) and High-Performance Products (HPP). The company's performance, strategic direction, and industry landscape provide an intriguing case for potential investors.

The TS segment operates through CSP’s subsidiary, Modcomp, Inc. in the United States and United Kingdom, and accounts for around 92% of CSP’s total revenue. It is a value-added reseller (VAR), selling third-party IT products, software, and hardware, while also providing consulting and managed services, mostly to small-to-medium-sized businesses (SMBs), particularly in sectors such as education, healthcare, and government.

The key revenue drivers within the TS segment are the firm’s reselling third-party IT infrastructure solutions, which are defined by partnerships with Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), Cisco (CSCO), Microsoft (MSFT), and Palo Alto Networks (PANW); professional IT consulting services, which involves planning, deployment, and integration services; and MSP, which is a growing component that refers to recurring revenue from IT infrastructure management and cloud solutions.

Although the TS segment contributes such an enormous amount to CSP’s total revenue, the segment is defined by low-margins, given the ferocious competition from larger VARs such as CDW (CDW) and Presidio.

The HPP segment contributes just 8% of total revenue and is focused on network security and cybersecurity software. Its flagship security product line is ARIA Software-Defined Security (SDS), which offers packet intelligence, threat detection, and managed security operations. The segment’s core products are its ARIA SDS; ARIA Zero Trust (AZT) PROTECT, which is designed to prevent high-profile attacks, especially targeting critical infrastructure sectors; and Myricom SmartNICs, which are a declining solution thanks to supply chain challenges, and which are basically network adapters for specialized low-latency applications.

Although it is clear that the cybersecurity industry is compelling, given the rise of artificial intelligence (AI), and the looming presence of quantum computing, alongside the continued increase in computing, all of which have increased the threat surface, CSP’s small market share, as well as competition from larger firms such as Palo Alto Networks and Fortinet (FTNT) make it highly unlikely that CSP will ever earn meaningful and sustainable profits.

Industry Structure and Competitive Landscape

Technology Solutions Industry

The IT infrastructure and managed services market is highly fragmented, with actors ranging from small VARs, like CSP to large national players such as CDW, Insight Enterprises, Inc. (NSIT), and Computacenter, given their economies of scale and strong vendor relationships. Pricing power is hard to attain, therefore, with firms trying to undercut each other with low-cost solutions, and this explains CSP’s thin margins in the market.

Although CSO differentiates itself through its expertise in complex, multi-vendor integration; strong partnerships with major IT providers; and emphasis on managed services for SMBs, there are not enough to resist margin pressure and ward off the risk of customer attrition to larger firms that enjoy superior pricing power.

Cybersecurity Industry

As aforementioned, the cybersecurity market is rapidly expanding given the rising capacity of malevolent actors, coupled with increasing use of computing as a function of growing populations and economic activity. The global cybersecurity market is projected to reach $203 billion worldwide this year, and grow by 7.58% a year between 2025 and 2029, reaching a size of $271.9 billion by 2029. The firm’s ARIA solutions compete with firms such as Palo Alto Networks, a leading firewall and cloud security solutions provider; CrowdStrike, which provides endpoint security leader; and Fortinet, which provides network security.

CSP differentiates itself through its managed detection and response (MDR) services, offered to critical infrastructure industries. Again, given the firm’s small size and therefore, small R&D budget, the firm is likely to struggle to break through and capture significant market share and earn pricing power.

Profitability is Hard to Sustain

The company’s net operating profit after tax (NOPAT), the calculations for which are in the table below, has waned between negative and positive returns since 2020, declining from -$0.6 million in 2020 to -$0.7 million in the trailing twelve months (TTM).
